If they had followed the protocol, we wouldn't be behind schedule now.
If Camille had escalated the shortage in March, the handover would be on schedule today.
If we weren't so exposed on compliance, I would have accepted the delay last week.
If I were an engineer who let scope creep slide, I would have signed that off months ago.
It was the missing checks that caused the delay.
Never have we seen a factory test logged so carelessly.
If the checks had been run, we wouldn't be renegotiating the date now.
We are going to have the three units retested at their cost.
By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan.

João, before we start, I want to be clear: this milestone is behind because Motiva released the cable schedule late. My team could not test what it could not access. So let us not turn this into a review of my factory.

Understood, Camille, and I hear you. Before we get into who owns what, could you run one thing by me? The inspection protocol we both signed in February — it lists three checks as mandatory hold points. Were those three run before the test was logged as a pass?
Two of them were run. The third we deferred, because the cable data was not there yet. It was a practical decision under your delay.

Then let me name the elephant in the room myself: our procurement team released the cable schedule late, and I own that. That is real. But a deferred check is not a passed check — and what the protocol requires is that a skipped hold point is logged as open, not as a pass.
That is a documentation point. The units are fine. Reopening the test now costs my team two weeks we do not have.

If that check had been run in February, we wouldn't be having this conversation now. It was that one deferred check that put us here, not the cable schedule alone. On compliance I cannot sign off on a deferred hold point, so we are going to have the three units retested against the full protocol.
And if I say the retest is not in my budget? What then?

Then I escalate it, and neither of us wants that. So here is what I would rather do: I own the cable delay in writing, you retest the three checks at your cost, and by the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan. You get a written status update from me every Friday until this package is signed off. Fair?
Let me name the elephant in the room: part of this delay is ours, and I own it.
It was the missing checks that caused the delay, not the cable schedule.
If your team had run the checks, we wouldn't be renegotiating this milestone now.
We are going to have the three units retested against the full protocol, at your cost.
By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan, and you will have a status update every Friday.

João, it's Marina, before your debrief call with Camille this afternoon, let me give you the state of play, because the numbers are not comfortable. We are at the halfway milestone, and on paper we are eight weeks behind on the signaling package. Here is what the record actually shows, and I want you to have it in front of you. The inspection protocol was signed by both sides in February. Camille's team then ran the factory acceptance test without three of the checks that protocol requires, and they logged it as a pass. If they had followed the protocol we agreed, we would not be arguing about compliance now, and the handover would still be on the original date. That is the leverage you have, and it is in writing, so use it calmly. Now, the elephant in the room. Part of this is ours. Our procurement team was late releasing the cable schedule, and if we had released it in March, some of their delay would have looked very different. Name that yourself before Camille does, because if she names it first, we lose the moral high ground. What I need from you by the end of the call is not blame. I need a corrective action plan, a weekly status update we can both see, and a realistic date for provisional acceptance that factors in the retesting. Escalate to me only if she refuses to retest the three failed checks. Everything else, you can close in that room. You have handled harder calls than this one. Good luck.

Can I tell you the one thing I wish somebody had told me at the halfway point of a career like yours? There is a gap, and almost nobody talks about it, between the English you RECOGNIZE and the English you USE. You have spent months now collecting structures. The third conditional, the causative, the cleft sentence, the inversion, the future perfect. When you read them, or when you hear a native speaker use them, you understand every one. That is recognition, and it is the easy half. The hard half is this: under pressure, in a room, with somebody defensive on the other side of the table, do those structures come OUT of your mouth, in the right order, without you reaching for them? That is use, and it is a completely different muscle. Here is the test I give people. Take one real fact from your project. Say it five different ways. If the supplier had followed the protocol, we would not be renegotiating the date. It was the missing checks that caused the delay. Never have we seen a factory test logged so carelessly. We are going to have the three units retested at their cost. By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan. Same fact, five structures, and every one of them was already in your head. The milestone review is not about learning something new. It is about turning everything you recognize into something you reach for without thinking. Do that, and the second half of the program is where your English stops being a subject you study and starts being a tool you forget you are holding.
